subject: Constitutional Law
lesson_title: Grounds and Procedure of Judicial Review

questions.question: Under Order 84 of the Rules of the Superior Courts 1986, an application for leave to seek judicial review must generally be made promptly and in any event within how many months of the date of the decision?
questions.explanation: Order 84, rule 21(1) RSC provides that an application for leave to apply for judicial review shall be made promptly and in any event within six months from the date when grounds for the application first arose. Shorter time limits apply in specific contexts (e.g., planning — eight weeks under the Planning and Development Act 2000). The court retains a discretion to extend time where there is good reason to do so.

questions.question: The threshold for obtaining leave to apply for judicial review in Ireland requires the applicant to demonstrate:

options.A: A prima facie case that the decision is unlawful
options.B: That it is arguable that a good ground for review exists (the arguable grounds standard)
options.C: A likelihood of success on the merits exceeding 50%
options.D: That the decision has caused actual damage to the applicant

questions.explanation: The leave threshold in Ireland is that the applicant must demonstrate arguable grounds — i.e., that there is an arguable case that the impugned decision is invalid. This is a relatively low threshold (G v DPP [1994] 1 IR 374). The purpose is to filter out clearly unmeritorious applications without imposing a full merits assessment at the leave stage.

questions.question: In State (Keegan) v Stardust Victims Compensation Tribunal [1986] IR 642, Henchy J articulated the Irish test for unreasonableness in judicial review. Which of the following best captures the Keegan/O'Keeffe standard?

options.A: The decision must be the most reasonable option open to the decision-maker on the evidence
options.B: The decision must be so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker could have made it — the 'irrationality' standard
options.C: The decision must be proportionate and necessary in a democratic society
options.D: The decision must conform to the requirements of natural justice and fair procedures

questions.explanation: The O'Keeffe v An Bord Pleanala [1993] 1 IR 39 / Keegan test is that a court may intervene where the decision is so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker would have reached it — sometimes described as 'irrationality' or 'manifest unreasonableness'. This is a high threshold that respects the decision-maker’s primary domain. It is distinct from the Wednesbury standard but in substance very similar.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recognised ground of judicial review in Ireland developed by the courts?

options.A: Proportionality — applicable to all administrative decisions involving any interference with rights
options.B: Legitimate expectation — where a public body has created a reasonable expectation through representation or practice
options.C: Reasonableness of outcome — courts assess whether the correct decision was made on the merits
options.D: Comparative deference — courts compare the decision with those of similar bodies in other jurisdictions

questions.explanation: Legitimate expectation is a well-established ground of Irish judicial review: where a public authority has made a clear and unambiguous representation (by promise, policy or practice) that it will act in a particular way, the court may enforce that expectation unless the public interest requires otherwise. See Glencar Exploration plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84 (SC) per Fennelly J, setting out the requirements for a successful legitimate expectation claim.

questions.question: Certiorari, mandamus and prohibition are the principal judicial review orders. Which of the following correctly matches each order with its function?

options.A: Certiorari quashes a decision; mandamus compels performance of a duty; prohibition prevents an unlawful act before it occurs
options.B: Certiorari prevents future action; mandamus quashes past decisions; prohibition grants a declaration of rights
options.C: Certiorari declares a decision void; mandamus orders the payment of damages; prohibition removes a decision-maker
options.D: Certiorari stays proceedings; mandamus awards costs against the decision-maker; prohibition suspends a statutory power

questions.explanation: Certiorari quashes (brings up and sets aside) a decision already made; mandamus commands a public body to perform a public duty it has failed to perform; prohibition prevents a body from proceeding with or completing an action it proposes to take unlawfully. A declaration (formerly a fourth prerogative remedy) states the legal position without directly compelling action. These are the classic remedies in O.84 RSC.

questions.question: In Meadows v Minister for Justice [2010] IESC 3, the Supreme Court held that where fundamental rights are at stake in an administrative decision, the proportionality principle applies as a ground of review. This means:

options.A: The court replaces the decision-maker’s assessment of proportionality with its own on all questions of fact and law
options.B: The court reviews whether the decision-maker properly considered proportionality and whether the restriction of rights is proportionate, applying a heightened standard of review
options.C: Proportionality only applies where the decision involves criminal sanctions
options.D: Proportionality review is limited to decisions of the Minister for Justice under immigration law

questions.explanation: Meadows v Minister for Justice [2010] IESC 3 extended the proportionality standard of review to administrative decisions that affect constitutional rights. Where fundamental rights are engaged, the O’Keeffe irrationality standard gives way to a more intensive proportionality review: the court examines whether the impairment of rights is proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. However, this remains a review for error of principle rather than a full merits reassessment.

questions.question: The rule against bias (nemo iudex in causa sua) in Irish administrative law requires what standard of review for apparent bias?

options.A: The decision-maker had a subjective belief that they were not biased
options.B: A reasonable and informed observer would have a reasonable apprehension of bias
options.C: The decision-maker had a direct financial interest in the outcome of the decision
options.D: The applicant can demonstrate that the outcome would have been different but for the bias

questions.explanation: The test for apparent bias in Irish law is the objective apprehension test: would a reasonable observer, with knowledge of the relevant facts and circumstances, have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to bear? (Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [2000] 4 IR 412 per Denham J; Dublin County Council v Balfe, unreported). Actual bias is not required — the appearance of impartiality matters.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the principle of audi alteram partem as applied in Irish judicial review?

options.A: Every person affected by an administrative decision must be given a full oral hearing with legal representation
options.B: A person whose rights or legitimate interests are adversely affected by a decision must be given prior notice and a fair opportunity to be heard
options.C: Both parties to a dispute must make written submissions before any decision can be made
options.D: Public authorities must publish draft decisions for public comment before making final determinations

questions.explanation: Audi alteram partem (hear the other side) requires that before a decision adversely affecting a person's rights or interests is made, that person must be given notice of the case against them and a fair opportunity to respond. The content of the required hearing varies with the nature of the decision and the interests at stake (the sliding scale): In Re Haughey [1971] IR 217; State (Gleeson) v Minister for Defence [1976] IR 280.

questions.question: An applicant seeks judicial review of a planning decision on grounds of irrationality. The An Bord Pleanála inspector’s report supported refusal; the Board granted permission without giving reasons for departing from the report. What is the most relevant judicial review ground?

options.A: Illegality — the Board had no power to grant permission contrary to its inspector’s recommendation
options.B: Failure to give reasons — where a decision departs from the inspector’s report, adequate reasons must be given
options.C: Proportionality — the decision must be the least restrictive option consistent with planning objectives
options.D: Legitimate expectation — the applicant was entitled to rely on the inspector’s recommendation as the outcome

questions.explanation: Irish law (O’Donoghue v An Bord Pleanála [1991]; Mulholland v An Bord Pleanála [2005]) requires adequate reasons when a planning authority departs from its inspector’s report. Reasons must be intelligible, adequate and deal with substantial matters in controversy. Failure to give reasons constitutes a ground of review under the duty to give reasons — an aspect of natural justice/fair procedures.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'legitimate expectation' in Irish law was authoritatively restated in Glencar Exploration plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84. Which of the following conditions did Fennelly J identify as necessary for a successful claim?

options.A: The representation must be made in writing by a senior official of the public body
options.B: The applicant must have suffered quantifiable financial loss in reliance on the representation
options.C: There must be a clear and unambiguous representation by the public body, actual or reasonably anticipated reliance, and it must be fair in all the circumstances to uphold the expectation
options.D: The representation must have been confirmed by the relevant Minister and published in Iris Oifigiúil

questions.explanation: Fennelly J in Glencar identified three elements: (1) a clear and unambiguous representation (by promise, practice or policy) by the public authority; (2) actual or reasonably anticipated reliance by the applicant on that representation; (3) it must be fair, in all the circumstances, to uphold the expectation (this includes considering the public interest). The remedy may be procedural (consultation before departure) or substantive (enforcement of the expectation).
